<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="df736ec0-773b-4d7b-b494-5bf26a96a41d"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1351312429</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="dba9df01-1863-4880-b7a8-b4e66232f8a0"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1351312429</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="49a4ffbb-faa5-4795-846e-a5e4d885ad80"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1351312429</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Besides generating Rich Text Format templates and Rich Text Format reports, Report Wizard also generates other types of document:</p><ol><li><em>*.html</em> – Hyper Text Markup Language</li><li><em>*.rtf</em> – Rich Text Format</li><li><em>*.odt</em> – OpenDocument Text</li><li><em>*.ods</em> – OpenDocument Spreadsheet</li><li><em>*.odp</em> – OpenDocument Presentation</li><li><em>*.docx</em> – Microsoft Word Document</li><li><em>*.xlsx</em> – Microsoft Excel Spreadsheet</li><li><em>*.pptx</em> – Microsoft PowerPoint Presentation</li><li><em>*.xml</em> – Extensible Markup Language</li><li><em>*.txt</em> – Text</li><li><em>*.docbook</em> or <em>*.dbk</em> DocBook</li></ol><p>To create a template for a specific type of document, simply open the editor designed for that particular type of document, and then save the template making sure that the filename extension is the same as that of the type of document.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="08106878-41a7-41d4-a67b-2c218aaf396e"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1351312428</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="61afca81-c526-470d-9918-a66345dd0b8e" /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169678873 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 01108: Supported HTML tags

- page_id: `169678873`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169678873/Supported+HTML+tags
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2018-06-08T14:38:23.048+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Report Wizard > Appendix D: HTML tag support
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Note

1040445681

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Note

1040445684

1040445678

**On this page**

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Note

1040445683

#### Font tags

A font tag consists of three attributes: **Size**, **Face**, and **Color**.

##### Size

The Size attribute determines the font size. Possible values are integers from 1 to 7. The default base font size is 3. The greater the value is, the larger the size becomes.

- The base font size for RTF documents is 24 dot (equivalent to 12 pt).
- The base font size for ODF documents is 12 pt.
- The base font size for OOXML documents is 12 pt.
- The base font size for HTML documents is determined by the web browser.

Each value will be multiplied by two. Shown below is an example of the size attribute:

```text
It will be rendered as font size 16 pt

It will be rendered as font size 12 pt

It will be rendered as font size 8 pt]]>
```

If the size attribute is specified without the face attribute, the default font will be determined by the template or document editor, unless the font tag is covered by other HTML elements, such as <code> or <tt>.

##### Face

The Face attribute defines the font name. If the face attribute is specified without the size attribute, the default size will be determined by the template or the document editor.

##### Color

The Color attribute specifies the text color. A color value can be either a hexadecimal number (prefixed with a hash mark) or one of the following sixteen colors. Colors are case-insensitive.

The table below lists several Font Colors:

| Color | Hexadecimal code |
| --- | --- |
| Black | #000000 |
| Silver | #C0C0C0 |
| Gray | #808080 |
| White | #FFFFFF |
| Maroon | #800000 |
| Red | #FF0000 |
| Purple | #800080 |
| Fuchsia | #FF00FF |
| Green | #008000 |
| Lime | #00FF00 |
| Olive | #808000 |
| Yellow | #FFFF00 |
| Navy | #000080 |
| Blue | #0000FF |
| Teal | #008080 |
| Aqua | #00FFFF |

For example:

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

As shown in the figure below, the outputs in *RTF, ODF,* or *HTML* will be:

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

#### Font style tag

The table below lists various Font Style Elements:

| Tag name | Description |
| --- | --- |
| TT | Renders teletyped or monospaced text. |
| I | Renders italic text. |
| B | Renders bold text. |
| BIG | Renders text in large font. |
| SMALL | Renders text in small font. |
| STRIKE and S | Renders strikethrough text. |
| U | Renders underlined text. |

- TT This tag will be rendered as <font face=”Courier New”>
- I This tag is supported by the existing HTML conversion component.
- B This tag is supported by the existing HTML conversion component.
- BIG This tag will be rendered as <font size=”5”>
- SMALL This tag will be rendered as <font size=”1”>
- STRIKE and S This tag will be rendered in a strikethrough text.
- U This tag is supported by the existing HTML conversion component.

#### Phrase elements

The table below lists assorted Phrase elements:

| Tag name | Function |
| --- | --- |
| EM | Indicates emphasis. |
| STRONG | Indicates stronger emphasis. |
| CITE | Contains a citation or a reference to other sources. |
| DFN | Indicates that this is the defining instance of the enclosed term. |
| CODE | Designates a fragment of computer code. |
| SAMP | Designates a sample output from programs, scripts, etc. |
| KBD | Indicates the text to be entered by the user. |
| VAR | Indicates an instance of a variable or program argument. |
| ABBR | Indicates an abbreviated form such as WWW, HTTP, URI, and Mass. |
| ACRONYM | Indicates an acronym such as WAC and radar. |

- *EM* 
This tag will be rendered as <i>
- *STRONG* 
This tag will be rendered as <b>
- *CITE* 
This tag will be rendered as <i>
- *DFN* 
This tag will be rendered as <i>
- *CODE* 
This tag will be rendered as <font face=”Courier New”>
- *SAMP* 
This tag will be rendered as <font face=”Courier New”>
- *KBD* 
This tag will be rendered as <font face=”Courier New”>
- *VAR* 
This tag will be rendered as <i>
- *ABBR* 
This tag will be rendered as normal text.
- *ACRONYM* 
This tag will be rendered as normal text.

#### Ordered and unordered lists and list Item tags

Ordered and unordered lists are rendered in an identical manner; however, ordered list items are numbered.

The report engine supports both unordered and ordered lists without attributes. The list tag attributes will be ignored in the report output. The list tag attributes are type, start, value, and compact.

Neither unordered nor ordered lists are supported in *XLSX* and *ODS* templates.

##### Ordered lists

An Ordered List is defined by the <OL> element. This element contains one or more <LI> elements that define the actual items of the list.

Unlike unordered lists (UL), items in an ordered list have a definite sequence. A conversion will render each item in the list with a number. All <OL> attributes will be ignored in the report output. An example of an Ordered List tag is shown in the figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

As shown in the figure below, the outputs in *RTF, ODF,* or *HTML* will be:

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Nested ordered lists

HTML conversion will indent nested lists with respect to the current level of nesting. Each level will begin at 1. An example of the Nested Ordered List tag is shown in the figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

As shown in the figure below, the outputs in*RTF, ODF,* or *HTML* will be:

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Unordered lists

An Unordered List is defined by the UL element. This element contains one or more LI elements that define the actual list items.

A conversion will render the UL element with a bullet preceding each list item. All UL attributes will be ignored in the report output. The figure below shows an example of an Unordered List tag:

[IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
 
As shown in the figure below, the outputs in *RTF, ODF*, or *HTML* will be:

##### [IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Nested unordered lists

Lists can also be nested. *HTML* conversion will indent nested lists with respect to the current level of nesting.

*HTML* conversion should attempt to present a small filled-in circle to the first level, a small circle outline to the second level, and a filled-in square to the third level. Bullets after the third level are filled-in squares. An example of the Nested Unordered List tag is shown in the figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

As shown in the figure below, the outputs in*RTF, ODF,* or *HTML* will be:

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

#### Definition list tags

A Definition List is defined by the DL element. An entry in the list is created using the DT element for the term being defined and the DD element for the definition of the term.

A definition list can have multiple terms for a given definition, as well as multiple definitions for a given term. Authors can also give a term without a corresponding definition, and vice versa, but such a structure rarely makes sense.

A conversion will render DT as a non-indent item and DD as a single indent item. The Definition List tag is not supported in the*XLSX* template. The figure below shows an example of a Definition List tag:

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

As shown in the figure below, the outputs in *RTF, ODF* or *HTML* will be:

#### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
 
Line and paragraph tags

A line break is defined by the <BR> element. This element inserts a single line break. It is an empty tag, meaning that it has no end tag. The line attributes will be ignored in the report output.

A paragraph is defined by the <P> element. The element automatically creates some space before and after itself. The paragraph attributes will be ignored in the report output. The figure below shows an example of a Line Break and Paragraph tag:

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

As shown in the figure below, the outputs in *RTF, ODF,* or *HTML* will be:

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

#### Preformatted text

A preformatted text is defined by the <PRE> element. All the space and carriage returns are rendered exactly as you type them. The preformatted attributes will be ignored in the report output. The figure below shows an example of the preformatted text:

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

As shown in the following two figures, the outputs in *RTF/ ODF* and *HTML* will be, respectively:

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

#### Heading tags

A heading is defined by the <H1>, <H2>, <H3>, <H4>, <H5>, or <H6> element. In this report, all heading tags will be rendered as <b> for*ODT, RTF,* and *OOXML* document outputs. The heading attributes will be ignored in the report output. The figure below shows an example of Heading tags.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

As shown in the following two figures, the outputs in *RTF / ODF* and *HTML* will be, respectively:

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

#### Link tags

A Link tag is defined by the <A> element. This element is used to create a link to another document with the href attribute. The href attribute specifies the destination of the link. Link tags are not supported in the *XLSX* template. The figure below shows an example of a Link tag:

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

As shown in the following three figures, the outputs in *RTF, ODF*, and *HTML* will be, respectively:

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

#### Table tags

A table is defined by the <TABLE> element. A table consists of multi-dimensional data arranged in rows and columns.

##### Table elements

The <TABLE> element takes a number of optional attributes to provide presentational alternatives in a document. The table attributes will be ignored in the report output, except for the following attributes:

- border

Specifies the width in unit of the border of a table.

- bgcolor

Specifies the table background color. Applying the background color here will affect the whole table.

Table elements are not supported in *XLSX, PPTX, ODS,* and *ODP* templates.

The figure below shows an example of table tags:

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

As shown in the following two figures, the outputs in *RTF / ODF* and *HTML* will be, respectively:

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**Border**

Border width in *HTML* is specified in pixels. When the table attributes are converted into *RTF*, *ODF*, or *OOXML*, 1 pixel will be equal to 1 pt.

**Color**

The attribute value type "bgcolor" refers to color definitions as specified in [SRGB]. A color value may be either a hexadecimal number (prefixed by a hash mark) or one of the following sixteen colors. Colors are case-insensitive.

The following table lists common Table Element Colors.

| Color | Hexadecimal Code |
| --- | --- |
| Black | #00000000 |
| Silver | #C0C0C0 |
| Gray | #808080 |
| White | #FFFFFF |
| Maroon | #800000 |
| Red | #FF0000 |
| Purple | #800080 |
| Fuchsia | #FF00FF |
| Green | #008000 |
| Lime | #00FF00 |
| Olive | #808000 |
| Yellow | #FFFF00 |
| Navy | #000080 |
| Blue | #0000FF |
| Teal | #008080 |
| Aqua | #00FFFF |

##### Row elements

The <TR> elements act as a container for a row of table cells. The <TR> elements must be contained within a <TABLE>.

<TR> contains <TH> or <TD> elements, which in turn contain the actual data of the table. <TR> takes presentational attributes for specifying the alignment of cells within the row and the row's background color. The row attributes will be ignored in the report output, except for the following attributes:

- align - Specifies the horizontal alignment for each cell in a row.
- valign - Specifies the vertical position of a cell's content.
- bgcolor - Specifies the table background color. A background color will apply to rows only (see Color in Table Elements for more details).

Row elements are not supported in *XLSX, PPTX, ODS*, and *ODP* templates.

**Align**

This attribute specifies the alignment of data and the justification of text in a cell. Possible values are:

- left - Left-flushed data/Left-justified text. This is the default value for table data.
- center - Centered data/Center-justified text. This is the default value for table headers.
- right - Right-flushed data/Right-justified text.
- justify - Double -justified text.
- char - No text alignment set.

**Valign**

This attribute specifies the vertical position of data within a cell. Possible values are:

- top: Cell data is flush with the top of a cell.
- middle: Cell data is centered vertically within a cell. This is the default value.
- bottom: Cell data is flush with the bottom of a cell.
- baseline: No text alignment set.

The figure below shows an example of TR tags:

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

As shown in the following two figures, the outputs in *RTF / ODF* and *HTML* will be, respectively:

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Cell elements

The <TD> elements define a data cell in a table. <TD> elements are contained within a <TR> element (a table row). The cell attributes will be ignored in the report output, except for the following attributes:

- align specifies the horizontal alignment for each cell in the row. See Align in Row Elements for more details.
- valign specifies the vertical position of a cell's contents. See Valign in Row Elements for more details.
- bgcolor specifies the table background color. A background color will apply only to cells. See Color in Table Elements for more details.
- rowspan rows spanned by the cell
- colspan columns spanned by the cell

Cell elements are not supported in *XLSX, PPTX, ODS,* and *ODP* templates.

**Row span**

This attribute specifies the number of rows spanned by the current cell. The default value of this attribute is one ("1"). For an *RTF* output, the result of row span (**.rtf*) is readable only in Word on Mac, and MS Word.

**Column span**

This attribute specifies the number of columns spanned by the current cell. The default value of this attribute is one ("1"). The figure below shows an example of a column span:

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

As shown in the following two figures, the outputs in *RTF / ODF* and *HTML* will be, respectively:

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Column width

Table column width can be adjusted via attributes and stylesheet. For example, a column's width can be set by its width attribute. Percentage or pixel units are allowed for each <TD> element. You may set table width in <table> tags by absolute pixel value or percentage of page ratio, then allocate width from the table to each column later.

For example:

```text
25%
                75%
       


        
                400px
                100px
        
 



	
		30px
		60px
		90px
	

 

	
		25%
		75%
	




Using Stylesheet:


	
		70%
		30%
	


]]>
```

DOCX, RTF and ODT templates retain their width values.The column width in the report is the same as defined in the Advanced HTML Editor dialog.

If the defined values exceed the paper margins, the table column widths will be converted to a percentage format so that the table appears within the page margins.

##### Header elements

The <TH> elements define a header cell in a table. <TH> elements are contained within a <TR> element (a table row). The header attributes will be ignored in the report output, except for the following attributes:

- align specifies the horizontal alignment for each cell in the row. See Align in Row Elements for more details.
- valign specifies the vertical position of a cell's contents. See Valign in Row Elements for more details.
- bgcolor specifies the table background color. A background color will apply to the whole table. See Color in Table Elements for more details.
- rowspan rows spanned by the cell. See Row span in Cell Elements for more details.
- colspan columns spanned by the cell. See Column span in Cell Elements for more details.

The default alignment for <TH> is center and the default font style for <TH> is bold. Header elements are not supported in *XLSX, PPTX, ODS,* and *ODP* templates. The figure below shows an example of header elements:

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

The following two figures show the outputs in *RTF / ODF* and *HTML,* respectively.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

#### Image tags

The <img> tag embeds an image in a document. The <img> tag consists of four attributes: **src**, **width**, **height**, and**alt**.

##### src

The src attribute specifies the location of an image resource. The value of this attribute can be one of the following types:

- A URL. The recognized scheme types are HTTP, HTTPS , and FILE
- An absolute path such as c:/user/image.png . The path separator can be either /(slash) or \(blackslash)

The output image format will depend on the format of the image source.

##### width

The width attribute specifies the width of an image in pixel units. For example, width = “100” or width = “100px”

##### height

The **height** attribute specifies the height of an image in pixel units. For example, height = “100”, or height = “100px”

#### NOTE: Note

Note

If the width or height attribute of an image is not specified, the size of the image will be calculated according to the following rules:

- For an image file that contains the width and height properties, such as JPG, PNG, and GIF , the size of the image output will be calculated from the size of the image.
- For an image file that has no width and height properties, such as SVG, EMF, and WMF , the size of the image output will be calculated from the size of the paper.

##### alt

The **alt** attribute specifies the alternate text of an image in case the image cannot be displayed.

#### NOTE: Note

Note

- The alternate text of image tags in DOCX, XLSX, and PPTX is available only in Microsoft Office.
- The alternate text of image tags in RTF files is shown only in the Description field, rather than the Title field in Microsoft Word and OpenOffice Writer.
- The alternate text of image tags in RTF files is not supported in LibreOffice.

In the event that the HTML code is as follows:

```text
]]>
```

The image will appear as shown in the figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

#### Superscript and subscript tags

##### Superscript

The <sup> tag defines a supersript, which is a very small letter, figure, or symbol, and printed above the line. Superscript characters can be used to write footnotes, like www[1], for example:

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Subscript

The <sub> tag defines a subscript, which is a very small letter, figure, or symbol, and printed below the line. For example, subscript characters can be used to write chemical formulas, such as H2O:

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1">Teal</td><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1">#008080</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1">Aqua</td><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1">#00FFFF</td></tr></tbody></table><p>For example:</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of Font Tags" ac:alt="Sample of Font Tags"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html01 - FONT1.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p>As shown in the figure below, the outputs in <em>RTF, ODF,</em> or <em>HTML</em> will be:</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of RTF, ODF, or HTML Document Outputs" ac:alt="Sample of RTF, ODF, or HTML Document Outputs"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html02 - FONT2.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h2>Font style tag</h2><p>The table below lists various Font Style Elements:</p><table class="wrapped" style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><colgroup><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><tr style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><th style="margin-left: 30.0px;">Tag <br />name</th><th style="margin-left: 30.0px;">Description</th></tr><tr style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><strong>TT</strong></td><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;">Renders teletyped or <br />monospaced text.</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><strong>I</strong></td><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;">Renders italic text.</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><strong>B</strong></td><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;">Renders bold text.</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1"><strong>BIG</strong></td><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1">Renders text in large <br />font.</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1"><strong>SMALL</strong></td><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1">Renders text in small <br />font.</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1"><strong>STRIKE and S</strong></td><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1">Renders strikethrough <br />text.</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1"><strong>U</strong></td><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1">Renders underlined <br />text.</td></tr></tbody></table><ul><li>TT <br />This tag will be rendered as &lt;font face=”Courier New”&gt;</li><li>I <br />This tag is supported by the existing HTML conversion component.</li><li>B <br />This tag is supported by the existing HTML conversion component.</li><li>BIG <br />This tag will be rendered as &lt;font size=”5”&gt;</li><li>SMALL <br />This tag will be rendered as &lt;font size=”1”&gt;</li><li>STRIKE and S <br />This tag will be rendered in a strikethrough text.</li><li>U  <br />This tag is supported by the existing HTML conversion component.</li></ul><h2>Phrase elements</h2><p>The table below lists assorted Phrase elements:</p><table class="wrapped" style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><colgroup><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><tr style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><th style="margin-left: 30.0px;">Tag <br />name </th><th style="margin-left: 30.0px;">Function </th></tr><tr style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><strong> EM</strong></td><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;">Indicates emphasis.</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><strong> STRONG</strong></td><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;">Indicates stronger emphasis.</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><strong> CITE</strong></td><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;">Contains a citation or a <br />reference to other sources.</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1"><strong>DFN </strong></td><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1"><p>Indicates that this is the defining <br />instance of the enclosed term.</p></td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1"><strong>CODE</strong></td><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1">Designates a fragment of computer <br />code.</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1"><strong>SAMP</strong></td><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1">Designates a sample output from <br />programs, scripts, etc.</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1"><strong>KBD</strong></td><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1">Indicates the text to be entered <br />by the user.</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1"><strong>VAR</strong></td><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1">Indicates an instance of a variable <br />or program argument.</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1"><strong>ABBR</strong></td><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1">Indicates an abbreviated form such <br />as WWW, HTTP, URI, and Mass.</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1"><strong>ACRONYM</strong></td><td style="margin-left: 30.0px;" colspan="1">Indicates an acronym such as <br />WAC and radar.</td></tr></tbody></table><ul><li><p><em>EM</em> <br />This tag will be rendered as &lt;i&gt;</p></li><li><p><em>STRONG</em> <br />This tag will be rendered as &lt;b&gt;</p></li><li><p><em>CITE</em> <br />This tag will be rendered as &lt;i&gt;</p></li><li><p><em>DFN</em> <br />This tag will be rendered as &lt;i&gt;</p></li><li><p><em>CODE</em> <br />This tag will be rendered as &lt;font face=”Courier New”&gt;</p></li><li><p><em>SAMP</em> <br />This tag will be rendered as &lt;font face=”Courier New”&gt;</p></li><li><p><em>KBD</em> <br />This tag will be rendered as &lt;font face=”Courier New”&gt;</p></li><li><p><em>VAR</em> <br />This tag will be rendered as &lt;i&gt;</p></li><li><p><em>ABBR</em> <br />This tag will be rendered as normal text.</p></li><li><p><em>ACRONYM</em> <br />This tag will be rendered as normal text.</p></li></ul><h2>Ordered and unordered lists and list Item tags</h2><p>Ordered and unordered lists are rendered in an identical manner; however, ordered list items are numbered.</p><p>The report engine supports both unordered and ordered lists without attributes. The list tag attributes will be ignored in the report output. The list tag attributes are type, start, value, and compact.</p><p>Neither unordered nor ordered lists are supported in <em>XLSX</em> and <em>ODS</em> templates.</p><h3>Ordered lists</h3><p>An Ordered List is defined by the &lt;OL&gt; element. This element contains one or more &lt;LI&gt; elements that define the actual items of the list.</p><p>Unlike unordered lists (UL), items in an ordered list have a definite sequence. A conversion will render each item in the list with a number. All &lt;OL&gt; attributes will be ignored in the report output. An example of an Ordered List tag is shown in the figure below.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of Ordered List Tag" ac:alt="Sample of Ordered List Tag"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Ordered list tag sample.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;">As shown in the figure below, the outputs in <em>RTF, ODF,</em> or <em>HTML</em> will be:</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="The Sample of RTF, ODF, or HTML Document Outputs" ac:alt="The Sample of RTF, ODF, or HTML Document Outputs"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html14 - OL2.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h3>Nested ordered lists</h3><p>HTML conversion will indent nested lists with respect to the current level of nesting. Each level will begin at 1. An example of the Nested Ordered List tag is shown in the figure below.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of Nested OL Tags" ac:alt="Sample of Nested OL Tags"><ri:attachment ri:filename="NestedOL1.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p> As shown in the figure below, the outputs in<em> RTF, ODF,</em> or <em>HTML</em> will be:</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="The Sample of RTF, ODF, or HTML Document Outputs" ac:alt="The Sample of RTF, ODF, or HTML Document Outputs"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html18 - NestedOL2.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></p><h3>Unordered lists</h3><p>An Unordered List is defined by the UL element. This element contains one or more LI elements that define the actual list items.</p><p>A conversion will render the UL element with a bullet preceding each list item. All UL attributes will be ignored in the report output. The figure below shows an example of an Unordered List tag:</p><p><ac:image ac:title="Sample of UL Tags" ac:alt="Sample of UL Tags"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Unordered list tag sample.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /> <br />As shown in the figure below, the outputs in <em>RTF, ODF</em>, or <em>HTML</em> will be:</p><h3 style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of RTF, ODF, or HTML Document Outputs" ac:alt="Sample of RTF, ODF, or HTML Document Outputs"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html06 - UL2.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h3><h3><br />Nested unordered lists</h3><p>Lists can also be nested. <em>HTML</em> conversion will indent nested lists with respect to the current level of nesting.</p><p><em>HTML</em> conversion should attempt to present a small filled-in circle to the first level, a small circle outline to the second level, and a filled-in square to the third level. Bullets after the third level are filled-in squares.  An example of the Nested Unordered List tag is shown in the figure below.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of Nested UL Tags" ac:alt="Sample of Nested UL Tags"><ri:attachment ri:filename="NestedUL1.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p>As shown in the figure below, the outputs in<em> RTF, ODF,</em> or <em>HTML</em> will be:</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of RTF, ODF or HTML Document Outputs" ac:alt="Sample of RTF, ODF or HTML Document Outputs"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html10 - NestedUL2.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br />  </p><h2>Definition list tags</h2><p>A Definition List is defined by the DL element. An entry in the list is created using the DT element for the term being defined and the DD element for the definition of the term.</p><p>A definition list can have multiple terms for a given definition, as well as multiple definitions for a given term. Authors can also give a term without a corresponding definition, and vice versa, but such a structure rarely makes sense.</p><p>A conversion will render DT as a non-indent item and DD as a single indent item. The Definition List tag is not supported in the<em> XLSX</em> template. The figure below shows an example of a Definition List tag:</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of DL Tags" ac:alt="Sample of DL Tags"><ri:attachment ri:filename="sample DL tags.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p>As shown in the figure below, the outputs in <em>RTF, ODF</em> or <em>HTML</em> will be:</p><h2><ac:image ac:title="Sample of RTF, ODF, or HTML Document Outputs" ac:alt="Sample of RTF, ODF, or HTML Document Outputs"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html22 - DL2.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /> <br />Line and paragraph tags</h2><p>A line break is defined by the &lt;BR&gt; element. This element inserts a single line break. It is an empty tag, meaning that it has no end tag. The line attributes will be ignored in the report output.</p><p>A paragraph is defined by the &lt;P&gt; element. The element automatically creates some space before and after itself. The paragraph attributes will be ignored in the report output. The figure below shows an example of a Line Break and Paragraph tag:</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of Line and Paragraph Tags" ac:alt="Sample of Line and Paragraph Tags"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html25 - LineAndParagraph1.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p>As shown in the figure below, the outputs in <em>RTF, ODF,</em> or <em>HTML</em> will be:</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="The Sample of RTF, ODF, or HTML Document Outputs" ac:alt="The Sample of RTF, ODF, or HTML Document Outputs"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html26 - LineAndParagraph2.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h2>Preformatted text</h2><p>A preformatted text is defined by the &lt;PRE&gt; element. All the space and carriage returns are rendered exactly as you type them. The preformatted attributes will be ignored in the report output. The figure below shows an example of the preformatted text:</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of Preformatted Text" ac:alt="Sample of Preformatted Text"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html29 - Preformatted1.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p>As shown in the following two figures, the outputs in <em>RTF/ ODF</em> and <em>HTML</em> will be, respectively:</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of RTF / ODF Document Output"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html30 - PRE2.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of HTML Document Output (Open in Internet Explorer 7.0)" ac:alt="Sample of HTML Document Output (Open in Internet Explorer 7.0)"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html32 - PRE4.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h2>Heading tags</h2><p>A heading is defined by the &lt;H1&gt;, &lt;H2&gt;, &lt;H3&gt;, &lt;H4&gt;, &lt;H5&gt;, or &lt;H6&gt; element. In this report, all heading tags will be rendered as &lt;b&gt; for<em> ODT, RTF,</em> and <em>OOXML</em> document outputs. The heading attributes will be ignored in the report output. The figure below shows an example of Heading tags.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of Heading Tags" ac:alt="Sample of Heading Tags"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html33 - HEADING1.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;">As shown in the following two figures, the outputs in <em>RTF / ODF</em> and <em>HTML</em> will be, respectively:</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of RTF / ODF Document Output"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html34 - HEADING2.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of HTML Document Output (Open in Internet Explorer 7.0)" ac:alt="Sample of HTML Document Output (Open in Internet Explorer 7.0)"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html36 - HEADING4.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h2>Link tags</h2><p>A Link tag is defined by the &lt;A&gt; element. This element is used to create a link to another document with the href attribute. The href attribute specifies the destination of the link. Link tags are not supported in the <em>XLSX</em> template. The figure below shows an example of a Link tag:</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of Link Tag" ac:alt="Sample of Link Tag"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html37 - LINK1.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p>As shown in the following three figures, the outputs in <em>RTF, ODF</em>, and <em>HTML</em> will be, respectively:</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of RTF Document Output" ac:alt="Sample of RTF Document Output"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html38 - LINK2.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html39 - LINK3.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of HTML Document Output (Open in Internet Explorer 7.0)" ac:alt="Sample of HTML Document Output (Open in Internet Explorer 7.0)"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html40 - LINK4.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h2>Table tags</h2><p>A table is defined by the &lt;TABLE&gt; element. A table consists of multi-dimensional data arranged in rows and columns.</p><h3>Table elements</h3><p>The &lt;TABLE&gt; element takes a number of optional attributes to provide presentational alternatives in a document. The table attributes will be ignored in the report output, except for the following attributes:</p><ul><li>border</li></ul><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;">Specifies the width in unit of the border of a table.</p><ul><li>bgcolor</li></ul><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;">Specifies the table background color. Applying the background color here will affect the whole table.</p><p><br /></p><p>Table elements are not supported in <em>XLSX, PPTX, ODS,</em> and <em>ODP</em> templates.</p><p>The figure below shows an example of table tags:</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of Table Tags" ac:alt="Sample of Table Tags"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html41 - Table1.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p>As shown in the following two figures, the outputs in <em>RTF / ODF</em> and <em>HTML</em> will be, respectively:</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of RTF Document Output" ac:alt="Sample of RTF Document Output"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html42 - Table2.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of HTML Document Output (Open in Internet Explorer 7.0)" ac:alt="Sample of HTML Document Output (Open in Internet Explorer 7.0)"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html44 - Table4.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p><strong>Border</strong></p><p>Border width in <em>HTML</em> is specified in pixels. When the table attributes are converted into <em>RTF</em>, <em>ODF</em>, or <em>OOXML</em>, 1 pixel will be equal to 1 pt.</p><p><strong>Color</strong></p><p>The attribute value type &quot;bgcolor&quot; refers to color definitions as specified in [SRGB]. A color value may be either a hexadecimal number (prefixed by a hash mark) or one of the following sixteen colors. Colors are case-insensitive.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;">The following table lists common Table Element Colors.</p><table class="wrapped" style="margin-left: 60.0px;"><colgroup><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody style="margin-left: 60.0px;"><tr style="margin-left: 60.0px;"><th style="margin-left: 60.0px;">Color</th><th style="margin-left: 60.0px;">Hexadecimal <br />Code</th></tr><tr style="margin-left: 60.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;">Black</td><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;">#00000000</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 60.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;">Silver</td><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;">#C0C0C0</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 60.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;">Gray</td><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;">#808080</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 60.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">White</td><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">#FFFFFF</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 60.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">Maroon</td><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">#800000</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 60.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">Red</td><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">#FF0000</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 60.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">Purple</td><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">#800080</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 60.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">Fuchsia</td><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">#FF00FF</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 60.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">Green</td><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">#008000</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 60.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">Lime</td><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">#00FF00</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 60.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">Olive</td><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">#808000</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 60.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">Yellow</td><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">#FFFF00</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 60.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">Navy</td><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">#000080</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 60.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">Blue</td><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">#0000FF</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 60.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">Teal</td><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">#008080</td></tr><tr style="margin-left: 60.0px;"><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">Aqua</td><td style="margin-left: 60.0px;" colspan="1">#00FFFF</td></tr></tbody></table><h3>Row elements</h3><p>The &lt;TR&gt; elements act as a container for a row of table cells. The &lt;TR&gt; elements must be contained within a &lt;TABLE&gt;.</p><p>&lt;TR&gt; contains &lt;TH&gt; or &lt;TD&gt; elements, which in turn contain the actual data of the table. &lt;TR&gt; takes presentational attributes for specifying the alignment of cells within the row and the row's background color. The row attributes will be ignored in the report output, except for the following attributes:</p><ul><li>align - Specifies the horizontal alignment for each cell in a row.</li><li>valign - Specifies the vertical position of a cell's content.  </li><li>bgcolor - Specifies the table background color. A background color will apply to rows only (see Color in Table Elements for more details).</li></ul><p>Row elements are not supported in <em>XLSX, PPTX, ODS</em>, and <em>ODP</em> templates.</p><p><strong>Align</strong></p><p>This attribute specifies the alignment of data and the justification of text in a cell. Possible values are:</p><ul><li>left -  Left-flushed data/Left-justified text. This is the default value for table data.</li><li>center - Centered data/Center-justified text. This is the default value for table headers.</li><li>right - Right-flushed data/Right-justified text.</li><li>justify - Double -justified text.</li><li>char - No text alignment set.</li></ul><p><strong>Valign</strong></p><p>This attribute specifies the vertical position of data within a cell. Possible values are:</p><ul><li>top: Cell data is flush with the top of a cell.</li><li>middle: Cell data is centered vertically within a cell. This is the default value.</li><li>bottom: Cell data is flush with the bottom of a cell.</li><li>baseline: No text alignment set.</li></ul><p>The figure below shows an example of TR tags:</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of TR Tags" ac:alt="Sample of TR Tags"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html45 - TR1.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p>As shown in the following two figures, the outputs in <em>RTF / ODF</em> and <em>HTML</em> will be, respectively:</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of RTF Document Output" ac:alt="Sample of RTF Document Output"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html46 - TR2.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of HTML Document Output (Open in Internet Explorer 7.0)" ac:alt="Sample of HTML Document Output (Open in Internet Explorer 7.0)"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html48 - TR4.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h3>Cell elements</h3><p>The &lt;TD&gt; elements define a data cell in a table. &lt;TD&gt; elements are contained within a &lt;TR&gt; element (a table row). The cell attributes will be ignored in the report output, except for the following attributes:</p><ul><li>align  <br />specifies the horizontal alignment for each cell in the row. See Align in Row Elements for more details.</li><li>valign <br />specifies the vertical position of a cell's contents. See Valign in Row Elements for more details. </li><li>bgcolor <br />specifies the table background color. A background color will apply only to cells. See Color in Table Elements for more details.</li><li>rowspan <br />rows spanned by the cell</li><li>colspan <br />columns spanned by the cell</li></ul><p>Cell elements are not supported in <em>XLSX, PPTX, ODS,</em> and <em>ODP</em> templates.</p><p><strong>Row span</strong></p><p>This attribute specifies the number of rows spanned by the current cell. The default value of this attribute is one (&quot;1&quot;). For an <em>RTF</em> output, the result of row span (<em>*.rtf</em>) is readable only in Word on Mac, and MS Word.</p><p><strong>Column span</strong></p><p>This attribute specifies the number of columns spanned by the current cell. The default value of this attribute is one (&quot;1&quot;). The figure below shows an example of a column span:</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of TD Tags As Column Spans" ac:alt="Sample of TD Tags As Column Spans"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html49 - TD1.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p>As shown in the following two figures, the outputs in <em>RTF / ODF</em> and <em>HTML</em> will be, respectively:</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of RTF / ODF Document Output"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html50 - TD2.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of HTML Document Output (Open in Internet Explorer 7.0)" ac:alt="Sample of HTML Document Output (Open in Internet Explorer 7.0)"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html52 - TD4.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h3>Column width<span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"> </span></h3><p>Table column width can be adjusted via attributes and stylesheet. For example, a column's width can be set by its width attribute. Percentage or pixel units are allowed for each &lt;TD&gt; element. You may set table width in &lt;table&gt; tags by absolute pixel value or percentage of page ratio, then allocate width from the table to each column later.</p><div><div class="syntaxhighlighter sh-confluence nogutter text"><p><br /></p><p>For example:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2f40341f-c2bc-43a3-b794-d695a88ed1c8"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[Using attributes:
<table width="500px">
        <tr>
                <td width="25%">25%</td>
                <td width="75%">75%</td>
       </tr>
</table>
<table width="500px">
        <tr>
                <td width="400px">400px</td>
                <td width="100px">100px</td>
        </tr>
</table> 


<table>
	<tr>
		<td width="30">30px</td>
		<td width="60">60px</td>
		<td width="90">90px</td>
	</tr>
</table>
 
<table>
	<tr>
		<td width="25%">25%</td>
		<td width="75%">75%</td>
	</tr>
</table>



Using Stylesheet:
<table style="width:50%">

	<tr>
		<td style="width:70%">70%</td>
		<td style="width:30%">30%</td>
	</tr>
</table>

]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></div></div><div><div class="syntaxhighlighter sh-confluence nogutter text"><div class="syntaxhighlighter sh-confluence nogutter text"><p><br /></p></div><p><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">DOCX, RTF and ODT templates retain their width values. </span><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">The column width in the report is the same as defined in the Advanced HTML Editor dialog.</span></p><p><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">If the defined values exceed the paper margins, the table column widths will be converted to a percentage format so that the table appears within the page margins.</span></p></div></div><h3>Header elements</h3><p>The &lt;TH&gt; elements define a header cell in a table. &lt;TH&gt; elements are contained within a &lt;TR&gt; element (a table row). The header attributes will be ignored in the report output, except for the following attributes:</p><ul><li>align <br />specifies the horizontal alignment for each cell in the row. See Align in Row Elements for more details.</li><li>valign <br />specifies the vertical position of a cell's contents. See Valign in Row Elements for more details. </li><li>bgcolor <br />specifies the table background color. A background color will apply to the whole table. See Color in Table Elements for more details.</li><li>rowspan <br />rows spanned by the cell. See Row span in Cell Elements for more details.</li><li>colspan <br />columns spanned by the cell. See Column span in Cell Elements for more details.</li></ul><p>The default alignment for &lt;TH&gt; is center and the default font style for &lt;TH&gt; is bold. Header elements are not supported in <em>XLSX, PPTX, ODS,</em> and <em>ODP</em> templates. The figure below shows an example of header elements:</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of TH Tags As Header Elements" ac:alt="Sample of TH Tags As Header Elements"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html53 - TH1.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p>The following two figures show the outputs in <em>RTF / ODF</em> and <em>HTML,</em> respectively.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of RTF / ODF Document Output"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html54 - TH2.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of HTML Document Output (Open in Internet Explorer 7.0)" ac:alt="Sample of HTML Document Output (Open in Internet Explorer 7.0)"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Html56 - TH4.PNG"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h2>Image tags</h2><p>The &lt;img&gt; tag embeds an image in a document. The &lt;img&gt; tag consists of four attributes: <strong>src</strong>, <strong>width</strong>, <strong>height</strong>, and<strong> alt</strong>.</p><h3>src</h3><p>The src attribute specifies the location of an image resource. The value of this attribute can be one of the following types:</p><ul><li>A URL. The recognized scheme types are <em>HTTP, HTTPS</em>, and <em>FILE</em></li><li>An absolute path such as<em> c:/user/image.png</em>. The path separator can be either /(slash) or \(blackslash) </li></ul><p>The output image format will depend on the format of the image source.</p><h3>width</h3><p>The width attribute specifies the width of an image in pixel units. For example, width = “100” or width = “100px”</p><h3>height</h3><p>The <strong>height</strong> attribute specifies the height of an image in pixel units. For example, height = “100”, or height = “100px”</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="629bf1ba-19df-4333-8d08-5f0d2dd35c2a"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>If the width or height attribute of an image is not specified, the size of the image will be calculated according to the following rules:</p><ul><li>For an image file that contains the width and height properties, such as <em>JPG, PNG,</em> and <em>GIF</em>, the size of the image output will be calculated from the size of the image. </li><li>For an image file that has no width and height properties, such as <em>SVG, EMF,</em> and <em>WMF</em>, the size of the image output will be calculated from the size of the paper.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3>alt</h3><p>The <strong>alt</strong> attribute specifies the alternate text of an image in case the image cannot be displayed.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f6493199-6b6b-485a-af51-db66ad772ff9"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul class="alternate"><li>The alternate text of image tags in DOCX, XLSX, and PPTX is available only in Microsoft Office.</li><li>The alternate text of image tags in RTF files is shown only in the Description field, rather than the Title field in Microsoft Word and OpenOffice Writer.</li><li>The alternate text of image tags in RTF files is not supported in LibreOffice.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><p>In the event that the HTML code is as follows:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8a88872e-6b09-49ee-8137-117fa047e987"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[<img src="file:/D:/Waterlilies.jpg" width="200" height="200" alt="Waterlilies"/>]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p>The image will appear as shown in the figure below.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:title="Sample of IMG Tag" ac:alt="Sample of IMG Tag"><ri:attachment ri:filename="add_img_2.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h2>Superscript and subscript tags</h2><h3>Superscript</h3><p>The &lt;sup&gt; tag defines a supersript, which is a very small letter, figure, or symbol, and printed above the line. Superscript characters can be used to write footnotes, like www<sup>[1]</sup>, for example:</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Sample of superscript.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Superscript output.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h3>Subscript</h3><p>The &lt;sub&gt; tag defines a subscript, which is a very small letter, figure, or symbol, and printed below the line. For example, subscript characters can be used to write chemical formulas, such as H2O:</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Sample of subscript.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Output of subscript.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Supported HTML tags" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169679633 space=MD2024xR1 version=2 -->
## PAGE 01109: Supported scripting languages

- page_id: `169679633`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169679633/Supported+scripting+languages
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-05-08T12:00:30.825+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Macro Engine Documentation
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Deprecated JavaScript Nashorn

1425328535

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Deprecated JavaScript Nashorn

1425328548

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Deprecated JavaScript Nashorn

1425328537

Macro Engine supports five scripting languages: BeanShell, JavaScript Rhino, Jython, Groovy, and JRuby.

**BeanShell** 
BeanShell is a lightweight scripting language for Java. The shipped version is 2.1.7. The advantage of using BeanShell is that its syntax is compatible with Java; therefore, you can use the code assistant feature in most Java IDE. The BeanShell syntax documentation is available at *[http://www.beanshell.org/docs.html](http://www.beanshell.org/docs.html)*.

**JavaScript** 
JavaScript is a scripting language based on Java syntax. The documentation is available at: 
*[http://docs.oracle.com/javase/8/docs/technotes/guides/scripting/nashorn/index.html](http://docs.oracle.com/javase/8/docs/technotes/guides/scripting/nashorn/index.html)* and *[https://developer.mozilla.org/en/Rhino_documentation](https://developer.mozilla.org/en/Rhino_documentation)*.

#### WARNING: Deprecated JavaScript Nashorn

Deprecated JavaScript Nashorn

The JavaScript Nashorn language has been removed from the modeling tool. Please use JavaScript Rhino instead. Learn more on the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Deprecated JavaScript Nashorn' space='MD2024xR1'] page.

**Jython** 
Jython, a successor of JPython, is a Python programming language implemented in Java. The documentation is available at *[http://wiki.python.org/jython](http://wiki.python.org/jython)*. Besides automating the modeling tool by using Jython scripting, the plugin development in the Jython programming language is also supported by the modeling tool. For more information on Jython, see JPython Scripting in the OpenAPI User Guide.

**Groovy** 
Groovy is an agile and dynamic language for the Java Virtual Machine. Macro Engine uses Groovy 2.0.1. The advantage of using Groovy is that its Java-like syntax seamlessly integrates with the existing Java source code and libraries. It supports many IDEs that provide code completion and debugging. BeanShell scripts can be easily moved to Groovy, with some modifications. For more information on Groovy, go to *[http://groovy-lang.org/](http://groovy-lang.org/)*.

**JRuby** 
JRuby is a 100% pure-Java implementation of the Ruby programming language. The documentation is available at *[http://jruby.org/documentation](http://jruby.org/documentation)*.

By default, JRuby is not included in the modeling tool, however, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Adding JRuby and installing Gems' space='MD2024xR1'].

1425328534

**Related pages**

- Using code completion to develop BeanShell scripts
- Using NetBeans IDE to develop Groovy Scripts
- Using Eclipse to develop Groovy scripts
- Installing Gems for JRuby
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Adding a scripting language to the modeling tool' space='MD2024xR1']

The BeanShell syntax documentation is available at <em><a href="http://www.beanshell.org/docs.html">http://www.beanshell.org/docs.html</a></em>.</p><p><strong>JavaScript</strong><br />JavaScript is a scripting language based on Java syntax. Learn more on the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Deprecated JavaScript Nashorn" /></ac:link> page.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><strong>Jython</strong><br />Jython, a successor of JPython, is a Python programming language implemented in Java. The documentation is available at <em><a class="external-link" href="http://jruby.org/documentation" rel="nofollow">http://jruby.org/documentation</a></em>.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="aef3b1ca-54cf-4c05-bd0b-539b04b3a8e0"><ac:rich-text-body><p>By default, JRuby is not included in the modeling tool, however, <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Adding JRuby and installing Gems" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[you can add it manually]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>. </p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="90b41327-03e2-458f-a2b6-540272e4c868"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1425328534</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul class="childpages-macro"><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024xR1/Using+code+completion+to+develop+BeanShell+scripts">Using code completion to develop BeanShell scripts</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024xR1/Using+NetBeans+IDE+to+develop+Groovy+Scripts">Using NetBeans IDE to develop Groovy Scripts</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024xR1/Using+Eclipse+to+develop+Groovy+scripts">Using Eclipse to develop Groovy scripts</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024xR1/Installing+Gems+for+JRuby">Installing Gems for JRuby</a></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Adding a scripting language to the modeling tool" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169677102 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 01110: Suspect Links

- page_id: `169677102`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169677102/Suspect+Links
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2022-04-06T13:25:41.001+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Model analysis
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

INLINE

Working on a server project requires up-to-date knowledge of all relevant project changes. Thus, we introduce a new set of validation rules for suspect link analysis. These rules help you track changes in Requirements that are linked to design elements (such as SysML Blocks, Test Cases, Use Cases, etc.) by Satisfy, Derive, Refine or Verify relationships, and stay informed about unlinked and deprecated Requirements in the model.

700500

INLINE

The following topics contain more information on Requirements tracking:

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Tracking elements affected by Requirement changes' space='MD2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Suspect links tracking options' space='MD2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Suspect links tracking options' space='MD2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Tracking elements affected by Requirement changes' space='MD2024xR1']

You can visit the page[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Suspect links semantics' space='MD2024xR1']to learn more about the topic.

You can also create your own suspect links customization to keep track of the changes in the elements of the specified type. To learn more, read the following topic:

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Customizing suspect links tracking' space='MD2024xR1']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="excerpt" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="cf37d8af-0679-4196-89c9-1c5cf9196ca1"><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Working on a server project requires up-to-date knowledge of all relevant project changes. Thus, we introduce a new set of validation rules for suspect link analysis. These rules help you track changes in Requirements that are linked to design elements (such as SysML Blocks, Test Cases, Use Cases, etc.) by Satisfy, Derive, Refine or Verify relationships, and stay informed about unlinked and deprecated Requirements in the model.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:structured-macro ac:name="widget" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="45ce9e8b-0c91-40c0-a720-b18d82d49e42"><ac:parameter ac:name="width">700</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="url"><ri:url ri:value="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nTEeNbELbHU" /></ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="height">500</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="excerpt" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="71ccda79-5db6-4d89-b553-95040c995478"><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p class="auto-cursor-target">The following topics contain more information on Requirements tracking:</p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Tracking elements affected by Requirement changes" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link ac:anchor="Tracking Deprecated Requirements"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Suspect links tracking options" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Tracking deprecated Requirements]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li><ac:link ac:anchor="Tracking Unlinked Requirements"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Suspect links tracking options" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Tracking unlinked Requirements]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li><p><ac:link ac:anchor="Solving untracked links issue"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Tracking elements affected by Requirement changes" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Solving untracked links issue]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></p></li></ul><p><span>You can visit the page </span><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Suspect links semantics" /></ac:link><span> to learn more about the topic.</span></p><p>You can also create your own suspect links customization to keep track of the changes in the elements of the specified type. To learn more, read the following topic:</p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Customizing suspect links tracking" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target" style="text-align: center;"><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169677125 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 01111: Suspect links semantics

- page_id: `169677125`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169677125/Suspect+links+semantics
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-01-29T14:46:45.214+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Model analysis > Suspect Links
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Enabling suspect links tracking applies the *<<track>>* stereotype to the relationships. It is applied to the relationships automatically right after enabling the suspect links tracking, changing the tracking scope, or creating a new relationship (in the case when suspect links are already enabled) if the following criteria are satisfied:

- The relationship type is Satisfy, Verify, Refine, or deriveReq. If custom suspect links tracking is created, the relationship type, direction, source, and target element types should be as defined in the configuration.
- The relationship itself and the relationship's source and target elements are under the specified tracking scope.

The stereotype contains *fingerprint* and *version* tags:

- fingerprint: is used to indicate that changes occurred at the end of a relationship. In other words, the fingerprint is used to highlight that relationship is suspected.
- version: is used to get the Teamwork Cloud server project version, which should be compared to the latest in order to identify what changes occurred in the linked element . In other words, the version is used when the action "Show Changes" is performed on a suspected link.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Upon enabling suspect links tracking, the stereotype *<<track>>*, which contains fingerprint and version tags, is applied tothe relationship 'Satisfy'.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Enabling suspect links tracking applies the <em style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">&lt;&lt;track&gt;&gt;</em> stereotype to the relationships. It is applied to the relationships automatically right after enabling the suspect links tracking, changing the tracking scope, or creating a new relationship (in the case when suspect links are already enabled) if the following criteria are satisfied:</p><ul><li>The relationship type is Satisfy, Verify, Refine, or deriveReq. If custom suspect links tracking is created, the relationship type, direction, source, and target element types should be as defined in the configuration.</li><li>The relationship itself and the relationship's source and target elements are under the specified tracking scope.</li></ul><p>The stereotype contains <em>fingerprint</em> and <em>version</em> tags:</p><ul><li>fingerprint: is used to indicate<span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"> that changes occurred at the end of a relationship. In other words, the fingerprint is used to highlight that relationship is suspected.</span></li><li><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">version: </span><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">is used to get the Teamwork Cloud server project version, which should be compared to the latest in order to identify what changes occurred in the linked element</span><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">. <span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">In other words, t</span><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">he version is used when the action &quot;Show Changes&quot; is performed on a suspected link.</span></span></li></ul><h6 style="text-align: center;margin-bottom: 30.0px;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="suspect_links_semantics.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Suspect links semantics" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> <br />Upon enabling suspect links tracking, the stereotype <em>&lt;&lt;track&gt;&gt;</em>, which contains fingerprint and version tags, <span style="color: rgb(94,108,132);">is applied to </span><span style="color: rgb(94,108,132);">the relationship 'Satisfy'</span>.</h6>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169677117 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 01112: Suspect links tracking options

- page_id: `169677117`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169677117/Suspect+links+tracking+options
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2021-10-12T14:03:59.509+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Model analysis > Suspect Links
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

767157416

767157437

767157414

**On this page**

**3**

767157427

##### Tracking Deprecated Requirements

Suspect links analysis allows tracking deprecated Requirements. This is useful when you are working with Requirements and some of them are no longer used. In that case, apply the *«deprecated»* stereotype to mark the Requirements of this type. All deprecated Requirements are provided in the **Validation Results** panel.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

To check whether deprecated Requirements are tracked

- In the main menu, go to Analyze > Suspect Links and select Options . If the Track Deprecated Elements option is set to true , tracking deprecated elements is enabled. [ATTACHMENT filename='track_deprecated_elements_option.png']

##### Tracking Unlinked Requirements

Suspect links analysis allows tracking unlinked Requirements. This is useful when you're working with structure elements and you need to know which Requirements remain unsatisfied.

To check whether unlinked Requirements are satisfied

1. In the main toolbar, go to Analyze > Suspect Links and select Options .
2. Click the arrow next to the Track Unlinked Requirements option and choose: a) All – if you want to track all the unlinked Requirements b) Excluding Owning – if you want to track only those unlinked Requirements, that have no sub-Requirements c) None – if you want to disable tracking unlinked Requirements. [ATTACHMENT filename='excluding_owning.png']
3. Click OK .

All unlinked Requirements are provided in the **Validation Results** panel.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Tracking Links in Main Project Only

You can limit the suspect links analysis scope to links contained in the main project only. If the option is enabled, used projects are excluded from the tracking scope.

To track links in the main project only

- In the main menu, go to Analyze > Suspect Links and select Options . If the Track Links in Main Project Only option is set to true , links are tracked in the main project only. [ATTACHMENT filename='track_links_in_main_project_only.png']

## PAGE 01113: Suspended Autochanges

- page_id: `169673074`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169673074/Suspended+Autochanges
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-05-19T13:19:18.900+02:00`
- ancestors: Suspended Symbols
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Abbreviation**

SuspendedAutochanges

**Description**

This validation rule checks if the diagram contains symbols whose content is not completely visible.

**Severity**

warning

**Constrained Element**

Diagram

**Solvers**

To fix this, make sure the symbols on the diagram are large enough to fit their content.

**Example**

###### **[IMAGE alt='' src='']**The diagram contains the Block *VCCU* which is too small to display its content, causing the error. 
The error is resolved once the Block *VCCU* is resized to fit its content.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p><strong>Abbreviation</strong></p><p>SuspendedAutochanges</p><p><strong>Description</strong></p><p>This validation rule checks if the diagram contains symbols whose content is not completely visible.</p><p><strong>Severity</strong></p><p>warning</p><p><strong>Constrained Element</strong></p><p>Diagram</p><p><strong>Solvers</strong></p><p>To fix this, make sure the symbols on the diagram are large enough to fit their content.</p><p><strong>Example</strong></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><strong><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Suspended_Autochanges.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Suspended Autochanges" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></strong>The diagram contains the Block <em>VCCU</em> which is too small to display its content, causing the error.<br />The error is resolved once the Block <em>VCCU</em> is resized to fit its content.</h6>
````

## PAGE 01114: Suspended Autoresizing

- page_id: `169673076`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169673076/Suspended+Autoresizing
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-05-19T13:01:48.751+02:00`
- ancestors: Suspended Symbols
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Abbreviation**

SuspendedAutoresizing

**Description**

This validation rule checks if the symbol content is not completely visible. Auto-resizing of shapes is suspended.

**Severity**

warning

**Constrained Element**

Diagram

**Solvers**

- Resize to Fit Content - resizes the symbol to fit its content.

**Example**

###### **[IMAGE alt='' src='']**The Block *VCCU* is too small to display its content, causing the error. 
The error is resolved via the **Resize to Fit Content**solver.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p><strong>Abbreviation</strong></p><p>SuspendedAutoresizing</p><p><strong>Description</strong></p><p>This validation rule checks if the symbol content is not completely visible. Auto-resizing of shapes is suspended.</p><p><strong>Severity</strong></p><p>warning</p><p><strong>Constrained Element</strong></p><p>Diagram</p><p><strong>Solvers</strong></p><ul><li><strong>Resize to Fit Content </strong>- resizes the symbol to fit its content.</li></ul><p><strong>Example</strong></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><strong><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Suspended_Autoresizing.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Suspended Autoresizing" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></strong>The Block <em>VCCU</em> is too small to display its content, causing the error.<br />The error is resolved via the <strong>Resize to Fit Content </strong>solver.</h6>
````
